sections.title: Key rule
sections.content:
When one verb is followed by another verb, you usually need **to + infinitive** or **verb-ing**.

You **cannot** simply use the base form alone (I hope see ❌).

Exceptions exist – see the 'Bare Infinitive' section later.

sections.title: Two main patterns
sections.content:
**To-infinitive:** I want to leave. She decided to apply.

**-ing:** I enjoy swimming. She finished reading.

The first verb decides which pattern you need.

sections.title: Exceptions: bare infinitive
sections.content: Some contexts use the **base form** without "to" (bare infinitive): after modal verbs (can, must, will…), after **make** / **let**, after verbs of perception (see, hear, watch…), and in fixed phrases like **'d better**, **'d rather**. Details in the Bare Infinitive section at the end.

sections.title: What are we comparing?
sections.content:
After verbs we use **to-infinitive** (to do) or **-ing** (doing). The verb decides which:

• **To-infinitive:** I want to leave. She decided to apply.
• **-ing:** I enjoy swimming. She finished reading.

Some verbs take only one; some take both (sometimes different meaning). Learn each verb's pattern.

For full verb lists, go to **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**.

sections.title: Verbs + to-infinitive (1)
sections.content:
These verbs are followed by **to + verb** (to do, to go):

• want, would like, hope, expect, decide, plan, agree, refuse, promise, offer, manage, fail, afford, need (to do), try (to do = attempt)
• After adjectives: happy to, glad to, pleased to, surprised to, easy to, difficult to

sections.title: Verbs + to-infinitive (2)
sections.content:
• After too/enough: It's too late to change. She's old enough to drive.
• Purpose: I came to see you. She phoned to ask.

sections.title: Verbs + to-infinitive (2)
sections.content:
Examples: I want to learn. She decided to stay. It's easy to forget.

For a complete list, see **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**.

sections.title: Verbs + -ing form (1)
sections.content:
These verbs are followed by **verb + -ing** (doing, going):

• enjoy, finish, avoid, consider, suggest, mind, keep, imagine, miss, practise, risk, deny, admit, delay, postpone
• After prepositions: interested in doing, good at doing, tired of doing
• After expressions: It's no use, It's worth, busy doing, can't help doing

sections.title: Verbs + -ing form (2)
sections.content:
Examples: I enjoy reading. She finished writing. Do you mind waiting?

For a complete list, see **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**.

sections.title: Verbs + both (same or similar meaning) (1)
sections.content:
Some verbs can take **to-infinitive** or **-ing** with little or no change of meaning:

• like, love, prefer, hate, start, begin, continue
• I like to swim / I like swimming. She started to cry / She started crying.

sections.title: Verbs + both (same or similar meaning) (2)
sections.content:
With **prefer** we often say: I prefer to do X rather than (do) Y, or I prefer doing X to doing Y.

For full lists (including verbs that take both), see **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**.

sections.title: Verbs + both (different meaning) (1)
sections.content:
With these verbs the **meaning changes**:

• **remember:** remember **to do** = don't forget (future). Remember **doing** = recall (past).
• **forget:** forget **to do** = fail to do. Forget **doing** = no memory of.
• **stop:** stop **to do** = pause in order to do. Stop **doing** = give up doing.

sections.title: Verbs + both (different meaning) (2)
sections.content:
• **try:** try **to do** = attempt. Try **doing** = experiment with.
• **regret:** regret **to do** = sorry to say (I regret to inform you). Regret **doing** = sorry about past.

sections.title: Verbs + both (different meaning) (2)
sections.content:
Examples: I remembered to lock the door. I remember meeting him. She stopped smoking.

For complete verb lists, see **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**.

sections.title: Other rules: prepositions and too/enough (1)
sections.content:
**After a preposition** → always **-ing**:

• interested **in** learning, good **at** swimming, tired **of** waiting
• look forward **to** seeing (here 'to' is a preposition)
• He left without saying goodbye.

sections.title: Other rules: prepositions and too/enough (2)
sections.content:
**After too / enough** → **to-infinitive**:

• It's too late **to** change. She's too young **to** drive.
• She's old enough **to** vote. There's enough time **to** finish.

These patterns apply regardless of the main verb.

sections.title: Quick summary (1)
sections.content:
• **Want, decide, hope, promise, need, try (attempt)** → to-infinitive.
• **Enjoy, finish, avoid, mind, suggest, consider** → -ing.
• **Like, love, start, begin** → both (similar meaning).
• **Remember, forget, stop, try, regret** → both but meaning changes (learn each pair).

sections.title: Quick summary (2)
sections.content:
• **After a preposition** → always -ing: interested in learning, good at swimming.
• **After too/enough** → to-infinitive: too late to change, old enough to drive.

sections.title: Quick summary (2)
sections.content:
Full verb lists: **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**.

See the reference table on the next page.

diagram.title: Verb patterns: to-infinitive vs -ing
rows: interested in, good at, tired of
too + adj + to, adj + enough + to

sections.title: Typical errors (1)
sections.content:
• **Want + -ing:** Wrong: I want *learning* English. Right: I want **to learn** English.
• **Enjoy + to:** Wrong: I enjoy *to read*. Right: I enjoy **reading**.
• **Look forward to:** Wrong: I look forward to *see* you. Right: I look forward to **seeing** you. (to = preposition → -ing)

sections.title: Typical errors (2)
sections.content: • **Stop:** Wrong: I stopped *drinking* water (meaning: I paused in order to drink). Right: I stopped **to drink** water.

sections.title: Typical errors (2)
sections.content: Tip: want/hope/decide → to-infinitive. enjoy/finish/avoid → -ing. For full lists, see **Reference → Infinitive and -ing form**.

sections.title: Bare Infinitive – When NO "to" is correct
sections.content:
• **Modal verbs** (can, must, will, should, may…): I can **swim**. She must **study**.
• **make / let:** They made me **laugh**. Let me **help**.
• **Verbs of perception** (see, hear, watch, feel, notice…): I saw her **leave**.
• **help** (both OK): Help me **carry** / **to carry** the bags.
• **Fixed phrases:** You'd better **go**, I'd rather **stay**.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Want is followed by to-infinitive, not -ing.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Enjoy is followed by -ing, not to-infinitive.

questions.question: 'I remember _____ the door.' (I recall doing it.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Remember doing = recall a past action. Remember to do = not forget to do something.

questions.question: 'I look forward to _____ you.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Look forward to is followed by -ing (to is a preposition here).

questions.question: 'She stopped _____.' (She gave up smoking.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Stop doing = give up. Stop to do = pause in order to do.

questions.question: 'She made him _____ the form.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Make + object + bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: 'I saw her _____ the room.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Verbs of perception (see, hear, watch) can take bare infinitive or -ing; bare infinitive often for the complete action.

questions.question: Why is 'I hope see you soon' wrong?

options.b: After hope we need to-infinitive, not the base form alone.
options.c: Hope is followed by that + clause only.

questions.explanation: When one verb follows another, we usually need to + infinitive or -ing; we cannot use the base form alone after hope.

questions.question: 'You'd better _____ now.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Fixed phrase 'd better is followed by bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: I want ____ (learn) Spanish.
questions.explanation: Want + to-infinitive.

questions.question: She enjoys ____ (read) in the evening.
questions.explanation: Enjoy + -ing.

questions.question: He decided ____ (apply) for the job.
questions.explanation: Decide + to-infinitive.

questions.question: Do you mind ____ (wait) a few minutes?
questions.explanation: Mind + -ing.

questions.question: We hope ____ (see) you soon.
questions.explanation: Hope + to-infinitive.

questions.question: She finished ____ (write) the report.
questions.explanation: Finish + -ing.

questions.question: I'm looking forward to ____ (meet) you.
questions.explanation: Look forward to + -ing (to is a preposition).

questions.question: He promised ____ (help) us. Use the verb in brackets in the correct form (infinitive with or without to).
questions.explanation: Promise + to-infinitive.

questions.question: They avoided ____ (answer) the question.
questions.explanation: Avoid + -ing.

questions.question: Don't forget ____ (lock) the door.
questions.explanation: Forget to do = fail to do (future).

questions.question: I'll never forget ____ (visit) Paris for the first time.
questions.explanation: Forget doing = no memory of (past experience).

questions.question: She suggested ____ (go) to the cinema.
questions.explanation: Suggest + -ing.

questions.question: She can ____ (swim) very well.
questions.explanation: Modal verbs (can, must, will…) take bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: They made us ____ (wait) for an hour.
questions.explanation: Make + object + bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: I heard him ____ (leave) early.
questions.explanation: Verbs of perception (hear, see, watch) can take bare infinitive.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I want learning English.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Want + to-infinitive, not -ing.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She enjoys to read.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Enjoy + -ing, not to-infinitive.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I look forward to see you.' Correct the sentence.
answers: I look forward to seeing you.
I look forward to seeing you
questions.explanation: Look forward to + -ing (to is a preposition).

questions.question: Wrong: 'He suggested to go early.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Suggest + -ing, not to-infinitive.

questions.question: Wrong: 'We decided staying longer.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Decide + to-infinitive, not -ing.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She finished to write the email.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Finish + -ing, not to-infinitive.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She made him to laugh.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Make + object + bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: Wrong: 'Let me to help you.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Let + object + bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Want is followed by to-infinitive, not -ing.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Enjoy is followed by -ing, not to-infinitive.

questions.question: 'I remember _____ the door.' (I recall doing it.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Remember doing = recall a past action. Remember to do = not forget to do something.

questions.question: 'I look forward to _____ you.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Look forward to is followed by -ing (to is a preposition here).

questions.question: 'She stopped _____.' (She gave up smoking.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Stop doing = give up. Stop to do = pause in order to do.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Want is followed by to-infinitive, not -ing.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Enjoy is followed by -ing, not to-infinitive.

questions.question: 'I remember _____ the door.' (I recall doing it.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Remember doing = recall a past action. Remember to do = not forget to do something.

questions.question: 'I look forward to _____ you.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Look forward to is followed by -ing (to is a preposition here).

questions.question: 'She stopped _____.' (She gave up smoking.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Stop doing = give up. Stop to do = pause in order to do.

questions.question: 'Let me _____ you with that.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Let + object + bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: 'I'd rather _____ at home tonight.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Fixed phrase 'd rather is followed by bare infinitive (no 'to').

questions.question: 'We watched them _____ the match.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Verbs of perception (watch, see, hear) take bare infinitive or -ing; bare infinitive for the complete action.

questions.question: 'He agreed _____ early.' Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Agree is followed by to-infinitive, not bare infinitive.
